entries.title: US hits Iran as Houthis claim strikes on Saudi tankers
entries.summary: The US is carrying out its 12th night of strikes on Iran. Meanwhile, Yemen's Houthis are claiming attacks on Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea. DW has the latest.
entries.content:
Iran

US hits Iran as Houthis claim strikes on Saudi tankers

Published July 23, 2026last updated July 23, 2026

The US is carrying out its 12th night of strikes on Iran. Meanwhile, Yemen's Houthis are claiming attacks on Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea. DW has the latest.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • US launches 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran
  • A US-Iran ceasefire deal fell apart on July 8
  • Yemen's Houthis attack Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea, say the tankers violated a naval blockade
  • Trump admin announces historic US-Saudi Arabic nuclear energy deal

Follow below for the latest developments on the US war with Iran and news from the wider Mideast on Thursday, July 23:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click hereto add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section READ: US and Saudi Arabia reach historic nuclear energy deal

READ: US and Saudi Arabia reach historic nuclear energy deal

The United States said on Wednesday it had signed a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, alongside a separate bilateral safeguards pact.

US Energy Secretary Chris Wright signed the agreements with Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman.

Skip next section Yemen's Houthis says they targeted Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea

Yemen's Houthis says they targeted Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea

Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement says it has targeted two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, escalating tensions around one of the world's key energy shipping routes.

The group identified the vessels as the Encelia and Layla, saying they were attacked with ballistic and cruise missiles for allegedly violating a Houthi naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, announced earlier this week.

A maritime security source told Reuters, the Saudi-flagged tanker Encelia sent a distress call after reporting it had been hit by a missile and caught fire near the Saudi port of Jizan.

Maritime security firm Vanguard and the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) also reported a tanker being struck by an unidentified projectile in the same area, causing a fire onboard. No casualties have been reported.

According to UKMTO, the ship’s master reported the incident approximately 70 nautical miles (130 kilometers) south-west of Al-Shuqaiq, Saudi Arabia.

The Houthis also claimed to have targeted a second vessel, Layla, though that report has not been independently confirmed.

The attack comes days after the Houthis announced a blockade of Saudi ports in the Red Sea, raising concerns about disruptions to global energy supplies. Ship-tracking data showed several tankers changing course on Wednesday following Houthi warnings to avoid Saudi ports.

Skip next section US launches 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran

US launches 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran

The US military says it has begun a 12th consecutive night of strikes against Iran, as the conflict shows no sign of easing.

US Central Command (CENTCOM), said the strikes aimed to reduce further Iran’s ability to threaten ships in regional waters amid tensions in the Strait of Hormuz.

The announcement came as US President Donald Trump escalated his rhetoric toward Tehran, warning that the United States would respond directly to any new attacks on vessels in the strategic waterway.

Trump said the US would destroy one bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack on a ship in the Strait of Hormuz.

The latest developments underline a growing focus on civilian and economic infrastructure as both sides continue to expand their military campaign around one of the world's most important energy and shipping routes.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage of the US war with Iran.

The US military has launched a 12th straight night of strikes on Iran as tensions continue to escalate.

Meanwhile, the US and Saudi Arabia have signed a civilian nuclear cooperation pact, accompanied by a separate bilateral safeguards agreement, US officials said.

Stay tuned as we bring you the latest on the US conflict with Iran and other developments from the Middle East.

entries.title: Iran war: Trump threatens bridges over Hormuz Strait attacks
entries.summary: US President Trump has threatened to destroy a "bridge or power plant" for each ship fired upon in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's foreign minister said any attack would "compel a powerful and decisive response."
entries.content:
United States of America

Iran war: Trump threatens bridges over Hormuz Strait attacks

Published July 22, 2026last updated July 23, 2026

US President Trump has threatened to destroy a "bridge or power plant" for each ship fired upon in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's foreign minister said any attack would "compel a powerful and decisive response."

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Trump says US will attack Iranian bridges and power plants if ships in Strait of Hormuz come under fire
  • Iran's foreign minister says his country's defense doctrine calls for 'an eye for an eye'
  • Fuel prices have soared as Iran continues to block the Strait of Hormuz and Houthis attack Saudi shipping in the Red Sea
  • Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth requests fresh funding for Iran war in remarks to senators
  • US attacked Iran for 11th consecutive night, CENTCOM says

This blog is now closed. For the latest on Thursday, please read here.  

Follow below for the latest developments on the US war with Iran and news from the wider Middle East:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Iran's foreign minister says its doctrine is 'eye for an eye'

Iran's foreign minister says its doctrine is 'eye for an eye'

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi issued a short statement in English on Wednesday evening threatening reprisals if the US attacks Iranian infrastructure.

"Our defense doctrine is clear: eye for an eye," Araghchi wrote. "Any aggression against Iran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response."

Iran is not in a position to strike at US infrastructure or targets on the mainland to match Trump's threats issued on Wednesday, and so Araghchi included a renewed warning to its regional neighbors.

"Those who contribute to such aggression, whatever the kind of support, will also be considered legitimate targets," he said.

Skip next section Iranian negotiator Qalibaf: 'no one will sell oil' in a region where we do not sell oil

Iranian negotiator Qalibaf: 'no one will sell oil' in a region where we do not sell oil

Iran's chief negotiator and parliamentary speaker, Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, has issued an online post saying that the "equation of this war is clear: either all or none!" 

He was making the comments soon after Trump's threat of attacking Iranian infrastructure if Iran attacks commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz. 

"In a region where we do not sell oil, no one will sell oil," Qalibaf wrote. "If our security is not ensured, no infrastructure will be safe, and the security of the strait is in the absence of American forces."

He concluded by saying Iran had "repeatedly" said the situation of the Strait of Hormuz would not "return to pre-war conditions," without specifying exactly what he meant. But it appears to be a reference to Iranian calls for the introduction of fees or at least some kind of authorization system for ships passing through the strait, even though it's on the open sea not an inland waterway. 

Skip next section Trump attends ceremony for 4 soldiers killed in action in region last week

Trump attends ceremony for 4 soldiers killed in action in region last week

US President Donald Trump has attended what's known as a "dignified transfer" ceremony, meeting the remains of four service members killed in action at the airfield where they were returned from the Middle East.

Trump, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff General Dan Caine all attended the ceremony at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware on Wednesday. The base is home to the largest US military mortuary.

Officials saluted as four cases draped with the Stars and Stripes flag were carried off a military transport plane and into vehicles waiting on the runway.

Trump was scheduled to meet privately with grieving family members. Asked what he was going to tell the families of the fallen, he said: "All I'm going to say is, we love you. We love your child, and that's what they are to them. They're their children. There's no ​games, no nothing."

The White House said one of the families will be on Air Force One later Wednesday when Trump flies to an event in Georgia, where the family lives.

Three of the soldiers were killed when an Iranian missile hit their barracks at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan on Friday. The other died in an incident in Erbil in Iraqi Kurdistan on Sunday, during a controlled detonation of an attack drone.

Skip next section Signs of ships avoiding second choke point off Yemen after Houthi warnings: tracker data

Signs of ships avoiding second choke point off Yemen after Houthi warnings: tracker data

Houthi threats to ships headed to or from Saudi Arabia's western shoreline could further impact commercial shipping, data trackers are warning. 

The Houthis, who have de facto control over much of Yemen, have declared a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia and told ships to avoid the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, the gateway to the Red Sea on the opposite side of the Arabian Peninsula from the Strait of Hormuz. It provides access to western Saudi coastal cities like Jeddah and Yanbu.

Data provider Kpler said shipping in both waterways was "under pressure," saying traffic in both straits had fallen sharply.

Ship broker Clarksons issued a similar warning, saying four vessels had registered new courses on Wednesday, avoiding the Bab el-Mandeb Strait and heading north through the Suez Canal towards the Mediterranean instead.

"The threat of Houthi hostile actions against Saudi Arabia ... has begun to have an overall effect on tanker activity," Clarksons wrote. "There are reports of several tankers, having ​loaded in Saudi Arabia's Yanbu port in the Red Sea, that have shifted course towards the Suez Canal rather than sail south towards the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, while ​others are holding their positions waiting for further instruction."

For ships headed to places like India, Pakistan, Indonesia or Australia, doing this would add well over 10,000 nautical miles (more than 20,000 kilometers) and weeks at sea to the typical travel distance.

"Anything that restricts shipping through the Red Sea would be problematic for the same reasons that the Strait of Hormuz is problematic," US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on the sidelines of a regional summit in the Philippines on Wednesday.

Pakistan's Foreign Ministry on Wednesday warned that the actions "threaten regional security, undermine freedom of navigation, challenge the rules-based maritime order and jeopardize uninterrupted flow of global commerce."

The Reuters news agency cited an advisory from the European Union's Aspides naval mission in the Red Sea as saying that ships with links to Israel, the United ​States or Saudi Arabia ‌were at a higher risk of being attacked by the Houthis and were advised to avoid voyages through the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden until the threat level decreases.

Skip next section Trump threatens to destroy 'bridge or power plant' for every attack on a ship in Strait of Hormuz

Trump threatens to destroy 'bridge or power plant' for every attack on a ship in Strait of Hormuz

US President Donald Trump has issued a social media post threatening that the US will start striking Iranian infrastructure in response to future attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz. 

"From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran," Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform.

Targeting key civilian infrastructure, such as power generation systems or bridges, is deemed a war crime in international humanitarian law, as is deliberately targeting civilian or commercial shipping.

Skip next section KLM airline extends halt to Middle East flights

KLM airline extends halt to Middle East flights

KLM Royal Dutch Airlines has extended its limits on flights to the Middle East amid the ongoing conflict. It said on its website that previous limits on flights to the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia would be extended from their previous end date of August 23.

"KLM will not operate flights to Dubai, Riyadh and Dammam until and including September 6," it said in updates on its website. 

The airline first restricted services on February 28 when the figthing first broke out and various airspaces in the region were closed.

Along with many other major airlines, KLM has periodically extended these limitations since, with the last such extension taking place on July 1.

Dubai in the UAE is close to the Strait of Hormuz, while Dammam is on Saudi Arabia's eastern coast near Bahrain. The capital, Riyadh, is further inland but still in the eastern half of the country.

Skip next section Saudi Arabia issues brief warning for Dammam, near Bahrain on east coast

Saudi Arabia issues brief warning for Dammam, near Bahrain on east coast

Saudi Arabia's civil defense agency briefly warned residents of the eastern city of Dammam to take shelter on Wednesday. 

Minutes later, the GDCD lifted the order and said the danger had passed.

Around the same time, air raid sirens were activated in Bahrain. 

Dammam is a coastal city on the Persian Gulf or Gulf of Arabia that's just a short distance from the island kingdom of Bahrain, with the two areas connected by the giant series of bridges known as the King Fahd Causeway.

Of the Gulf states with ties to the US, Iran has tended to fire at the major player, Saudi Arabia, less frequently amid the conflict, more typically attacking sites in Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar or the United Arab Emirates.

Skip next section Iran denies conducting nuclear activities at 'Pickaxe Mountain'

Iran denies conducting nuclear activities at 'Pickaxe Mountain'

Iran has denied engaging in nuclear activities at the site known as "Pickaxe Mountain" in the center of the country.

In a post on X, Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Ismail Baghai described US reports on the matter as "nothing more than a fabricated pretext for aggression, destruction, and sabotage."

He also added that Iran’s nuclear activities have been fully reported to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA).

US President Donald Trump threatened to launch attacks on a fortified underground site known as "Pickaxe Mountain," located in the Kuh-e Kolang mountain range, south of the Natanz nuclear facility in central Iran.

"We’ll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily," Trump told reporters on Tuesday.

Skip next section Brent oil price soars above $95 a barrel

Brent oil price soars above $95 a barrel

Amid the escalating conflict in the Middle East, world oil prices soared on Wednesday. The main contract, futures for Brent North Sea crude, surpassed $95 (€83) per barrel for the first time in nearly six weeks.

Brent oil jumped 4.4% to $95.02 per barrel, while the key US contract, West Texas Intermediate, rallied 4.5% to $88.16.

The US attacked Iran overnight from Tuesday to Wednesday for the eleventh consecutive night. Meanwhile, on Tuesday, the Houthis threatened to attack oil tankers calling at Saudi ports on the Red Sea.

The Red Sea has become the main alternate route for millions of barrels of Saudi oil per day, as Iran has threatened shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

Skip next section Pakistan condemns Houthis' threats against Saudi Arabia

Pakistan condemns Houthis' threats against Saudi Arabia

Pakistan expressed deep concern over attempts to draw Saudi Arabia into the war in the Middle East and condemned threats by Yemen's Houthi rebels against the kingdom and shipping in the Red Sea.

"Pakistan is deeply concerned by attempts to drag the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia into the conflict in the Middle East, and it reiterates its unwavering support for the security, sovereignty, territorial integrity, and prosperity of the brotherly Kingdom of Saudi Arabia," said the Pakistan's Foreign Ministry in a statement.

It also said the country reserves the right to use force to protect its own interests.

The move comes as the Yemen's Houthis, who control the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia.

Pakistan, a nuclear power, maintains a far-reaching military alliance with Saudi Arabia.

Skip next section Lebanon PM calls for 'complete Israeli withdrawal'

Lebanon PM calls for 'complete Israeli withdrawal'

Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam said that Beirut is working toward a "complete Israeli withdrawal" from occupied areas during a visit to a village in the south of the country where the Lebanese army recently deployed.

"We will continue mobilizing our political and diplomatic efforts to secure a complete Israeli withdrawal from the south," he said in Zawtar al-Gharbiya.

This battered village is one of several "pilot zones" intended to demonstrate the Lebanese army's ability to remove and disarm the Iran-backed Hezbollah as part of a Washington-sponsored deal with Israel.

Salam's visit to the village comes a day after President Joseph Aoun's meeting with US President Donald Trump at the White House, where Aoun said he "stressed the urgent need for a full Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory."

Trump promised to support the Lebanese government's efforts to establish state control throughout the country.

According to the agreement signed last month, the Lebanese army is supposed to disarm Hezbollah militants in the "pilot zones" where it will operate. This will ban military activity by any non-state groups and should pave the way for future Israeli withdrawals from other areas.

However, the Iran-backed group has repeatedly rejected the agreement and refuses to lay down its weapons.

Skip next section Trump to witness return of US soldiers killed in Middle East

Trump to witness return of US soldiers killed in Middle East

President Donald Trump on Wednesday will attend a ceremony for four US service members who were killed in Iranian attacks in the Middle East. Their bodies will return to American soil in flag-draped caskets.

Trump will travel to Dover Air Force Base in Delaware for the ceremony, known as a "dignified transfer."

The three US service members who were killed when an Iranian missile hit their sleeping barracks at the Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan on Friday were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25; Sergeant Angel S. Rampersad, 28; and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19.

Sergeant Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, died on Sunday in a separate incident in Erbil, Iraq, during a controlled detonation of an attack drone.

Eighteen US service members have been killed since the Iran war began on February 28.

Skip next section Jordan army says it intercepted four Iranian missiles

Jordan army says it intercepted four Iranian missiles

The Jordanian army said it intercepted four Iranian missiles, while two others fell in an open area.

"Air defence systems today detected six missiles coming from Iranian territory," the military said in a statement.

They added that they "intercepted and downed four missiles, while the other two fell in two remote, uninhabited areas without causing any human casualties or material damage."

Earlier, the Iranian army said it targeted accommodations and equipment storage facilities at the Al-Azraq Air Base in Jordan.

Skip next section Iran executes one more person over January protests

Iran executes one more person over January protests

Iran has executed a man on charges linked to protests in January, according to the judiciary's Mizan news agency.

Mizan identified the man as Mehdi Khaneki and said he was executed for "operational actions" that benefited Israel, the United States, and hostile groups. The agency also reported that he produced and possessed weapons.

The report did not specify the exact time of the execution. To date, the Iranian judiciary has executed at least 25 men in connection with the protests and subsequent unrest according to the German DPA news agency.

Demonstrations broke out in Iran amid the country’s economic crisis in late December. These demonstrations quickly escalated into mass protests against the authoritarian leadership. However, the Iranian government violently suppressed the protests, killing thousands of demonstrators.

Skip next section Iran hits US bases in Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Iranian media report

Iran hits US bases in Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Iranian media report

The Iranian army said it targeted US forces in Jordan,  Bahrain and Kuwait, according to reports from the Iranian state-run media.

The army said it attacked with drones accommodations and equipment storage facilities at the Al-Azraq Air Base in Jordan, as well as equipment warehouses and aircraft maintenance hangars at the Sheikh Isa Air Base in Bahrain.

The Iranian army also reportedly launched a large-scale drone strike against US military facilities at Camp Doha in western Kuwait. The attack targeted ammunition storage sites and logistics infrastructure supporting American ground forces.

There has been no confirmation of these attacks by the US yet.

entries.title: US and Saudi Arabia reach historic nuclear energy deal
entries.summary: The deal is another sign of growing US-Saudi ties during US President Donald Trump's second term. The agreement, known as a 123 Agreement, would allow Saudi Arabia to build nuclear reactors using US technology.
entries.content:
Saudi Arabia

US and Saudi Arabia reach historic nuclear energy deal

July 23, 2026

The deal is another sign of growing US-Saudi ties during US President Donald Trump's second term. The agreement, known as a 123 Agreement, would allow Saudi Arabia to build nuclear reactors using US technology.

The United States said on Wednesday it had signed a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, alongside a separate bilateral safeguards pact.

US Energy Secretary Chris Wright signed the agreements with Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman.

The US Energy Department said the agreements would deepen cooperation between Washington and Riyadh in the civilian nuclear energy sector. 

“Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decadeslong, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation,” the US Energy Department said in a statement.

Deal allows nuclear reactors and uranium enrichment

The agreement, known as a 123 Agreement, would allow Saudi Arabia to build nuclear reactors using US technology.

It would also permit the Kingdom to enrich uranium and reprocess nuclear waste, activities that can potentially produce material for nuclear weapons.

However, like all signatories to the nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty, Saudi Arabia has already committed to not developing an atomic bomb and consented ‌to international inspections.

The deal, however, does not include an Additional Protocol that would allow the UN nuclear watchdog, the International Atomic Energy Agency, to conduct more intrusive inspections at short notice.

The US administration said the agreement complied with non-proliferation requirements under the Atomic Energy Act.

Washington and Riyadh have discussed nuclear cooperation for years, with Saudi Arabia seeking access to US technology and American companies competing for major contracts.

What happens next?

The agreement will now be submitted to Congress and will take effect unless lawmakers vote to block it within 90 legislative days.

A two-thirds majority would be required to override a presidential veto.

Several Democratic lawmakers have raised concerns that the agreement could increase proliferation risks and trigger a regional arms race.

“They are allowing Saudi Arabia, a belligerent and authoritarian nation, to develop nuclear weapons technologies while starting a war with Iran under the guise of preventing an Iranian nuclear bomb,” Democratic Senator Edward Markey told Reuters.

“This deal would make us all less safe,” he added.

“This agreement will set off a nuclear race in the region, further disincentivizing Iran from limiting its own program,” Democratic Senator Chris Murphy said on X.

Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman has previously said the kingdom does not want to develop a nuclear weapon but would do so if Iran acquired one.

Saudi Arabia wants nuclear energy to reduce domestic oil use, leaving more crude available for the energy-rich Kingdom to export. Harnassing nuclear power would help Saudi Arabia slash emissions and generate power for energy-intensive services such as desalination and air conditioning.

Edited by: Wesley Dockery

entries.title: US judge sets Maduro trial date for June 2027
entries.summary: US prosecutors accuse Maduro of using the power of the Venezuelan state to protect and promote illegal drug smuggling.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

US prosecutors accuse Maduro of using the power of the Venezuelan state to protect and promote illegal drug smuggling.

A US judge on Wednesday set a date for Venezuelan former President Nicolas Maduro and his wife, Cilia Flores, to go on trial in New York, where they will face charges of drug trafficking.

The two have been detained since early January, when US forces entered Venezuela and seized them from their Caracas home.

The unprecedented middle-of-the-night raid was hailed by US President Donald Trump as a "surgical law enforcement operation" in a criminal case, which had been brought against the couple six years ago.

Maduro has maintained his innocence, calling himself a prisoner of war and referring to his capture as a kidnapping.

Judge Alvin Hellerstein set June 1, 2027, as the trial date for Maduro and his wife, following a joint request from prosecutors and defense counsel.

Hellerstein also scheduled an upcoming hearing for November 17 to discuss defense motions, including a challenge to the legality of how Maduro and Flores were captured and brought to the United States.

"The ‌parties all ​agree that this is a realistic schedule. We don't think it's going to slip, but obviously if anything comes up we will inform your honor," Maduro's lawyer Barry Pollack told the judge on Wednesday.

Maduro faces life in prison

US prosecutors have accused Maduro of using the power of the Venezuelan state to protect and promote illegal drug smuggling. He has also been accused of forming alliances with cartels to bring tons of cocaine into the United States.

If found guilty, Maduro and Flores face potential life prison sentences.

The pair sat through the 15-minute hearing separately at a defense table, surrounded by their lawyers. They did not speak during the proceedings. But Maduro waved to an unknown person in the courtroom's gallery as he was led out of the courtroom by authorities.

Pollack said his team plans to challenge the indictment on sovereign immunity grounds, saying that if successful, Maduro won't have to continue litigating the case.

Since Maduro was removed from power, Venezuela has been led by his former Vice President Delcy Rodriguez.

Rodriguez reestablished bilateral relationships with the US, with Washington effectively taking over control of Venezuela's oil exports.

In turn, the US has eased sanctions on the country's oil industry, while foreign companies have expanded oil and gas projects.

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

Edited by: Sean Sinico

entries.title: Hungary: Ex-PM Orban pledges resistance to Magyar 'tyranny'
entries.summary: Hungary's former prime minister alleges that his successor has "dismantled the rule of law and eliminated checks and balances." Peter Magyar has pursued Viktor Orban and his allies since winning April's elections.
entries.content:
Hungary

Hungary: Ex-PM Orban pledges resistance to Magyar 'tyranny'

July 22, 2026

Hungary's former prime minister alleges that his successor has "dismantled the rule of law and eliminated checks and balances." Peter Magyar has pursued Viktor Orban and his allies since winning April's elections.

Hungary's former Prime Minsiter Viktor Orban accused prosecutors and the new government of sham investigations into his Fidesz party on Wednesday, a day after investigators seized equipment from the party HQ in Budapest as part of a corruption probe. 

Hungary's longstanding leader, Orban lost April's general elections in a landslide to conservative rival Peter Magyar, himself a former Fidesz member, in a result hailed by much of Europe but not by the Trump White House. 

Since coming to power, Magyar has moved to dismantle what he alleges to be systemic corruption of the Orban and Fidesz era, making a host of sweeping changes. Orban, however, on Wednesday, called these measures signs of "tyranny" and said that he and his opposition party would offer "resistance" by all peaceful means. 

What did Orban say?

Orban's online statement late on Tuesday night, followed by his press conference outside the Fidesz HQ on Wednesday, constituted two of his strongest political statements since returning to a role as leader of the opposition in early May after 16 years in power.

On Facebook, he first issued what he called a "statement of resistance" soon after his party announced that prosecutors had searched the party's HQ and seized computer servers and other materials. This is part of a corruption probe into the country's National Culture Fund, which is alleged to have been a state-run payment center for Orban's inner circle and a shadow election campaign fund.

"The National Culture Fund issue was just a pretext for this [search of the party headquarters]," Orban alleged, denying that there had been any wrongdoing.

He said Hungary had "ceased to be a democratic state" after the new center-right government used its parliamentary supermajority to pass a constitutional amendment ending the mandates of the president and the head of the Constitutional Court — both deemed to be Orban allies. He said he and his party would "employ all peaceful means" to oppose an "illegitimate" political system.

On Wednesday, outside Fidesz's base of operations, Orban said he believed Magyar was trying to hobble his party once and for all.

"By yesterday, I had come to realize what this was all about," he told reporters. "What happened was that this served as a pretext to seize Fidesz's servers ... This is a politically motivated legal proceeding designed to render Fidesz — which has clearly survived even this heavy political defeat and is growing stronger — legally unviable."

How and when did Magyar win power?

Peter Magyar's new party, Tisza, comfortably won general elections on April 12, claiming more than 55% of the vote against Fidesz's tally of almost 37%.

Because of Hungary's first-past-the-post electoral system, Magyar now enjoys the same supermajority that Orban often did in his pomp, holding 141 of 199 seats despite having only won 55% of the votes. Fidesz holds just 52 seats.

In Hungary, a two-thirds vote in parliament is needed to amend the constitution.

The new prime minister, who had broken away from Fidesz in 2024, lamenting double standards and institutional corruption, took office on May 9. He had pledged rapid reforms if victorious, but the speed and scope of his actions even took some of his supporters by surprise.

What has Magyar done since taking office to Orban and Fidesz's detriment?

As well as taking steps in foreign policy like trying to mend ties with Brussels and free up assistance for Ukraine since taking office, Magyar has moved quickly against alleged Orban-era corruption and misdeeds. Magyar often refers to this as targeting Orban's "mafia." 

Magyar opened up former government facilities to the public to showcase some of the opulence he says indicates systemic corruption.

The national public broadcaster M1, which Tisza and others in opposition said had morphed into Fidesz's mouthpiece, was briefly taken off air this month and broadcast a black full-screen apology to launch a reform process.

Several political appointees have been removed from their roles — most notably President Tamas Sulyok via a constitutional amendment and Constitutional Court president Peter Polt. Magyar this week suggested chess star Judith Polgar for the largely ceremonial role as president, only for her to indicate that she was flattered but not interested.

On Tuesday, Prosecutor General Gabor Balint Nagy, appointed only last year, agreed to step down after being called Orban's "puppet" by Magyar.

Tisza has also introduced term limits of 12 years for all lawmakers, including the prime minister and government ministers. Orban says this means most of the veterans in his party, himself included, will be excluded from the next elections.

He told reporters in Budapest on Wednesday that this harkened back to the late 1980s and the latter days of Communist dictatorship "when there was no democracy in Hungary, there was no rule of law."

Corruption investigations, including but not limited to the investigation into the National Culture Fund, have also struck Orban and his allies.

Some other senior Fidesz members have voluntarily stepped out of the political limelight. Last week, parliamentary caucus leader Gergely Gulyas stepped down, while Orban's longstanding former Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto left to take up an executive position with Chinese electric carmaker BYD.

Surveys suggest that Orban's party has suffered even more in its first few months in opposition, with one poll by Median this week showing 60% support for Tisza and only 18% for Fidesz.

Edited by: Sean Sinico

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Louvre's Apollo Gallery reopens, 9 months after heist
entries.summary: The 17th-century Apollo Gallery reopened to the public without precious jewels or other objects previously on display.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

The 17th-century Apollo Gallery reopened to the public without precious jewels or other objects previously on display.

Paris' Louvre Museum reopened its Apollo Gallery to the public on Wednesday, marking the first time visitors were allowed in since Ocotber 2025, when robbers stole €88 million ($102 million) worth of crown jewels in a dramatic daytime heist.

The Louvre is world's most visited museum, with guest numbers of up to 9 million a year.

It was originally a royal palace, which was opened to the public during the French Revolution. The museum houses the Mona Lisa, one of the world's most famous pieces of art.

The October 2025 heist shocked the world as the thieves used a crane mounted on a truck to enter the museum through a window at 9:30 a.m., when the museum was still opening.

The group broke in through the window, equipped with power tools like a disc cutter, and stole French crown jewels.

Precious jewels relocated

On their way out, the thieves dropped a 170-year-old crown commissioned by Napoleon III as part of a pair of crowns for him and his wife, Empress Eugenie.

The crown was left "badly deformed," the Louvre said, but it was nevertheless "nearly intact" and could be restored. The crown was among other objects that were not taken from the gallery.

Museum operators said the Apollo Gallery will return to its 17th-century original ceremonial purpose, without any of the precious jewels or other objects on display.

The public will be able to tour the majestic room and view its gilded ceilings and Greek frescoes.

The Louvre's director, Christophe Leribault, said the former exhibits have been relocated elsewhere in the museum.

"It's also an opportunity to rediscover the splendid architecture and decor of this gallery," Leribault said.

The Apollo Gallery is considered one of the most marvelous areas of the Louvre, with ceilings designed by Charles Le Brun and later added to by, among others, French painter Eugene Delacroix.

A 'trauma for the entire world'

Leribault took over from former director Laurence des Cars, who resigned in the aftermath of the heist, which highlighted a string of security failures within the museum.

French Culture Minister Catherine Pegard referred to the heist as a "sinister day," adding that it cannot be forgotten.

"It was a trauma for the entire world, which, for several days, expressed its shock and sadness in every language. It was a trauma for us, discovering the fragility of a place we thought unshakeable, and which, like so many others, proved vulnerable," Pegard said.

Authorities have boosted security at the museum, having added new surveillance cameras and anti-intrusion systems.

Security improvements are part of the museum's decade-long modernization plan, estimated to cost up to €800 million.

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

Edited by: Sean Sinico

entries.title: Who could be the next UN Secretary-General?
entries.summary: For the first time in a decade, the United Nations will soon choose a new Secretary-General. DW takes a look at the six candidates, what they stand for and the challenges they and the UN will face.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

For the first time in a decade, the United Nations will soon choose a new Secretary-General. DW takes a look at the six candidates, what they stand for and the challenges they and the UN will face.

After a decade in power, Antonio Guterres, will end his second term as UN Secretary-General on December 31. There are currently six candidates lining up to replace him. Whoever emerges victorious has a tough task, with the relevance, power and financial capability of the UN under an increasingly harsh spotlight.

The UN Secretary-General is "equal parts diplomat and advocate, civil servant and CEO," according to the organization itself. The holder of the position oversees several vital UN organs, including the Security Council, charged with maintaining peace and security.

The winning candidate will be selected by the UN's General Assembly upon the recommendation of the Security Council (whose permanent members are China, France, Russia, the United Kingdom, and the United States), making the path to victory narrow and fraught.

Here's what you need to know about each candidate ahead of a debate between them on Thursday (July 23) at the UN's General Assembly Hall in New York.

Michelle Bachelet

The first female president of Chile (from 2006 to 2010 and then 2014 to 2018) is vying to become the first female UN Secretary-General, after several women lost out to Guterres in 2016. "If I am polite, I would say the world was not prepared for it. Is it prepared now? I hope so," Bachelet, 74, said earlier this year. "It will be a very good signal," she said, adding that it would provide hope for many people.

Bachelet also served as the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights between 2018 and 2022, a position which saw her involved in many of the key geopolitical issues she would face if elected.  While she has admitted she has no "magic formula" to resolve the issues on the Security Council, Bachelet has sought to talk up her experience as a key asset.

Rafael Mariano Grossi

The Argentine diplomat has spent most of his career in the nuclear and atomic energy sectors, culminating in his 2019 appointment as director general of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), an autonomous organization within the UN.

Given the geopolitical landscape, with nuclear war perceived as an increasing threat, Grossi sees his background as "clearly an advantage. You have to understand the world you live in," he told Swiss outlet Geneva Solutions.

Grossi supports the UN's current attempt at reform known as UN80 and says there is "no need to reinvent the wheel" but wants to streamline the organization with mergers of UN entities and reductions in "duplications, triplications, even quadruplications or quintuplications of structures," which add significant costs to members.

Maria Fernanda Espinosa

The Spanish-born Ecuadorian has packed plenty into her 61 years. As well as a stint as Ecuador's foreign minister, Espinosa is a poet, essayist and an expert on sustainable development and climate change, particularly in the Amazon region, where she worked with indigenous communities at the beginning of her career.

Espinosa acknowledged the UN's budget difficulties in an interview with UK newspaper The Observer. "The biggest challenge right now is the financial challenge," said Espinosa, who was also president of the UN General Assembly. As a result, she says, the UN must "reform, transform or die."

To achieve such reform, Espinosa wants to work more collaboratively with regional organizations, and lean on Europe, both in financial terms and as a "bridge-builder" between states with conflicting interests and desires.

Rebeca Grynspan

Vice President of her native Costa Rica between 1994 and 1998, Grynspan is currently Secretary-General of the UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), which advocates for developing countries in trade deals. Her work on negotiating a deal between the warring parties for the continued export of Ukrainian grain after the Russian invasion in 2022 has been widely praised.

Grynspan wants the UN to be bolder. "Bureaucracies are very risk-averse by nature, and you cannot have a good idea or take risk," she said at a recent event hosted by think tank Chatham House in London. She has also called for wider representation on the Security Council, particularly from Africa or Latin America.

Macky Sall

While other candidates have held prominent political positions, Sall's experience is the most recent. The 64-year-old was president of Senegal from 2012 to 2024, having previously served as the country's prime minister.

During his presidential tenure, Senegal saw significant infrastructure improvements. But Sall's popularity took a downward turn in 2021, during his second term, after protests against the imprisonment of opposition leader, Ousmane Sonko. Sall's unsuccessful attempt to postpone the 2024 presidential election, which triggered further deadly protests, has left him a divisive figure in his homeland.

Senegal's current government did not initially support Sarr's candidacy for the UN role but u-turned last month to back his bid.

Carolyn Rodrigues Birkett

The final candidate to throw their hat in the ring, Rodrigues Birkett entered the race in mid-June. She is the permanent representative of Guyana to the United Nations and has previously undertaken roles in both the UN and as Guyana's foreign minister, Rodrigues Birkett is an Amerindian (the Indigenous peoples of the Americas) and has also held governmental roles related to that community.

Guyana's president Irfaan Ali recently spelt out her UN ambitions.

"Ambassador Rodrigues Burkett's vision for the United Nations centers on strengthening multilateralism, enhancing effectiveness and responsiveness of the organization, promoting inclusive global governance and ensuring the United Nations remains capable of addressing contemporary challenges," he said.

Edited by: Rob Mudge

entries.title: How Russia is luring foreign nationals to fight in Ukraine
entries.summary: More and more, Russia is recruiting men from the world’s poorest countries to send them to the front in the Ukraine war. Why are people joining up?
entries.content:
Russian Federation

How Russia is luring foreign nationals to fight in Ukraine

July 22, 2026

More and more, Russia is recruiting men from the world’s poorest countries to send them to the front in the Ukraine war. Why are people joining up?

Charles Mutoka, a 72-year-old Kenyanis in mourning for his son, Oscar Khagola Mutoka, a former soldier who traveled to Russia in the summer of 2025 to look for work.

His father said that he had tried in vain to talk him out of it. Oscar did not tell him and the rest of his family exactly where he was going but a week after leaving he sent a picture of himself in uniform and never got back in touch again.

His family found out that he had died some time later but, despite appeals to the Kenyan authorities, they were not able to recover the body.

In May 2026, the family held a symbolic memorial service and set up a makeshift burial site for Oscar, in order to at least have a place where they could pray. "The worst part is that I don't know if and where he was actually buried, or if his body was simply dumped in a forest in Russia," Charles Mutoka said.

Oscar Mutoka is one of an estimated 27,000 or more foreign nationals recruited by Russia for its war in Ukraine,according to the Defense Intelligence ofUkraine (HUR). A joint study conducted by the International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH), the Truth Houndshuman rights organization and the Kazakhstan International Bureau for Human Rights and Rule of Law (KIBHR) provides evidence that Russia has set up a global network for recruiting foreign nationals.

"This recruitment targets people in economically, socially and legally precarious life situations. The methods employed range from enticing offers to deception and even coercion," Ilya Nusov, one of the authors of the study, told DW.

'I don't want to starve to death in Cuba'

For many people living in the world's poorest regions, the wages offered by the Russian army is a huge incentive as it means a chance to escape poverty and support their families. That's exactly why Yoan Viondi Mendoza, a Cuban national, decided to work in Russia. He was promised $2,000 (€1,750) per month, an enormous sum by Cuban standards, where the average wage is $20 (€17,50). 

DW found out about Yoan Mendoza's story from his brother Michael Duro, who lives in the US. Yoan had wanted to emigrate to the US and be reunited with his brother, but this turned out to be impossible, because of financial and bureaucratic hurdles.

Then he came across a job offer in Russia, his brother said. "Yoan called and said he'd found a way out. He told me about a 'project' in Russia," said Duro, who told DW that he had tried in vain to change his brother's mind.

In September 2023, the Reuters news agency filmed young Cubans who wanted to go to Russia, including Yoan Mendoza who was 23 at the time. He told journalists that he knew exactly where he was going and that he did not want to starve to death in Cuba. He also showed a document that was purportedly a contract with the Russian army, which would also give him Russian citizenship.

Duro said that his brother was sent to the front in Ukraine and then tried to escape. "He pleaded, 'I made a mistake. Forgive me. Please get me out of here quickly,'" Diro said, adding that afterwards there was no contact. Yoan's name later surfaced on the lists of foreign nationals killed in action that were published by Ukraine.

'They used us as human shields'

Though Mendoza and Oscar Mutoka probably knew that they were going to Russia to fight in the Ukraine war, many other foreign nationals are being deceived to the front after being promised jobs in construction, security, logistics or agriculture.

That's what happened to Arman Mondol from Bangladesh. He wanted to work abroad to lift his family out of poverty. His father, a farmer, sold some property and took out a loan to pay a recruiter. Mondol was told that he would be working as a packer in a warehouse. Equipped with a tourist visa, he traveled via Saudi Arabia to Russia, where he was then forced to sign a contract in Russian, which he could not read.

Mondol said that he and other foreign nationals had been treated badly in the Russian army. "When we were on a mission, they always sent us ahead while they remained behind us. They used us as human shields and pushed us into the thick of the fighting," he told DW.

During one of those missions, his vehicle hit a landmine. He was one of only two survivors and was taken to a military hospital, from where he managed to escape. In the end , the Bangladeshi embassy in Moscow helped him to return home, but the war continues to haunt him.

"I still have these images in my mind — dying people, their eyes, their disfigured faces. How they get blown up by landmines. How there was one drone attack after the next. How people's limbs were torn off. It was horrible," Mondol, who still has shrapnel in one leg and has a heavy limp, recalled. Back in his village, he has not found work.

Mercenaries or human trafficking?

Foreign nationals fighting for Russia in Ukraine are often called mercenaries. However, Ilya Nusov explained that, strictly speaking, this isn't what they are according to international law, since often people adopt Russian citizenship before joining the army.

While others had no intention of fighting in the first place. They could be considered the victims of human trafficking, if they are recruited on false premises and coerced. This violates the United Nations Convention against Transnational Organized Crime, and the Palermo Protocol, which bans human trafficking and has been ratified by most countries in the world, including Russia.

Russia has insisted that all foreign nationals partaking in the "special military operation," as it calls its war in Ukraine, are involved on a voluntary basis. In March, Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov claimed during a press conference with his Kenyan counterpart that "no one is being hired, just as no one is being recruited in the case of the special military operation. Volunteers get there in full compliance with Russian legislation." 

DW asked the Russian Foreign Ministry for a statement but had not received a response by the time of publication.

Increasing number of recruited foreign nationals

The involvement of foreign nationals in the war is receiving increased attention, with their families protesting from  Nepal to Peru. Some politicians, including Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi, have even brought up the subject in meetings with Russian President Vladimir Putin. According to media reports, Russia restricted the recruitment of citizens from various "friendly" countries in February 2026. A list of those countries has not been published.

"In some cases, this has contributed to reducing or even stopping the influx of fighters from certain countries at least temporarily," said Nusov. "But we believe that Russia also has ways to circumvent this by reducing recruitment in one place and focusing on a different country." According to Ukraine's Coordination Headquarters for the Treatment of Prisoners of War, Russia could recruit up to 18,500 foreign nationals in 2026.

Benjamin Alvarez Gruber, Nahid Anjuman, Felix Maringa contributed to this article, which was originally published in Russian.

entries.title: Zelenskyy talks with Trump envoys amid attacks on Russia
entries.summary: Ukraine's president said he discussed reinvigorating diplomacy with US envoys Witkoff and Kushner. The talk followed strikes by Ukraine's military on warehouses belonging to Wildberries — known as "Russia's Amazon."
entries.content:
Ukraine

Zelenskyy talks with Trump envoys amid attacks on Russia

July 22, 2026

Ukraine's president said he discussed reinvigorating diplomacy with US envoys Witkoff and Kushner. The talk followed strikes by Ukraine's military on warehouses belonging to Wildberries — known as "Russia's Amazon."

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said he spoke with US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner on Wednesday in an attempt to revive the prospects of a diplomatic end to Russia's invasion.

"I just spoke with President Trump's envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner," Zelenskyy said on X. "It was a good, important conversation on how to reinvigorate diplomacy and bring peace closer. Peace is needed — peace with dignity, and Ukraine has long been ready for it."

The discussion came after Ukrainian attacks on Wildberries, Russia's largest online retailer. The company said Wednesday that several people were injured in Ukrainian attacks on the company's warehouses.

Vladimir Kondratyev, the governor of southern Krasnodar said one person was killed in Wednesday's Ukraine strikes, but did not clarify whether that was the strikes at Wildberries or elsewhere.

What do we know about the latest Ukrainian attack on Wildberries?

Wildberries co-founder and CEO Tatyana Kim said facilities in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk were targeted in Wednesday's attacks.

Social media footage showed smoke billowing above the Krasnodar logistics site, which Kim said had been evacuated along with a facility in Nevinnomyssk.

Zelenskyy addressed the attacks on X, without naming Wildberries.

"Today, Ukraine's long-range sanctions successfully struck important targets in Russia's Krasnodar and Stavropol regions – logistics hubs involved in supplying the Russian army with drone components, navigation equipment, and other gear – as well as another oil depot," Zelenskyy wrote.

Wednesday's attacks follow the deaths of eight employees in strikes that destroyed Wildberries facilities on Saturday.

"There are no ⁠words ​to describe all the feelings and emotions caused by ​the attacks on ordinary people, on our employees, on all of us who are simply doing our ​jobs," Kim said in a ‌post on the Telegram messaging app.

Ukraine expands defense to target Russia's civilian economy

Wildberries, often called the Amazon of Russia, is a key component of the country's civilian economy. Kyiv also accuses it of providing military logistics and shipping for the country's military.

Online sales in Russia totaled 11.5 trillion Rubles ($147 billion, €129 billion) in 2025 according to the country's Association of Internet Trade Companies, a 28% increase over 2024.

Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak said sales of goods and services on digital platforms equated to around 8.5% of GDP.

Wednesday's strikes represent a widening trend of Ukrainian attacks deep within Russia as Moscow continues to target civilian infrastructure in the  invasion it launched against neighboring Ukraine on February 24, 2022.

Beyond its expanded attacks on Russia's civilian economy, the commander of Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces announced that Kyiv's military had also struck 13 Russian vessels in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov over the past 48 hours. 

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred news source on Google by clicking here, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: DW correspondent Alican Uludag released, ordered not to leave Turkey
entries.summary: Alican Uludag was freed Wednesday, a day after being taken back into custody on new charges. The arrest had come just two months after he was released pending trial in a separate case.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

Alican Uludag was freed Wednesday, a day after being taken back into custody on new charges. The arrest had come just two months after he was released pending trial in a separate case.

Deutsche Welle correspondent Alican Uludag was released Wednesday under judicial supervision after spending approximately 25 hours in police custody.

He was taken into custody Tuesday on suspicion of inciting hatred and hostility and publicly spreading misleading ​information the Turkish prosecutor's office ‌said.

At the time of his latest detention, Uludag was already on conditional release after being detained in February on charges including insulting President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Germany criticizes renewed detention

Before his release Wednesday, a German government spokesperson said Berlin was "highly irritated" by Uludag's renewed detention.

According to Reuters news agency, the spokesperson said Turkish authorities were acting in violation of press freedom, which Turkey had committed itself to upholding by joining the Council of Europe and signing the European Convention on Human Rights.

Following Tuesday's arrest, DW Director General Barbara Massing described his re-arrest as "deeply concerning" and added, "we expect his rights to be fully respected and demand a fair trial in accordance with the rule of law."

Journalists protest outside courthouse

Uludag's latest detention drew criticism from Turkish press organizations, which gathered outside the Ankara courthouse before the proceedings Wednesday.

A small group of demonstrators carried signs and chanted, "Journalism is not a crime," while calling for his immediate release.

Uludag was released, but the court warned him not to leave the country and ordered him to report to police four times a week.

Sinan Tartanoglu, head of the Ankara branch of the Turkish Journalists' Union, said the case reflected broader pressure on reporters in Turkey.

"There are many journalists in prison and many under investigation," Tartanoglu was quoted by DW's Turkish Service as saying. "We want to be able to report the news, publish our social media posts and express our criticism freely."

Uludag has repeatedly denied the charges against him.
